request.question: What was the population at the 2010 census of the city where the transmitter site for KQSP (1530 AM) is located?

title: Odessa, Texas
Sudislavsky District
Wentzville, Missouri
Roanoke, Indiana
Chanhassen, Minnesota
Kursk
Athens, Georgia
Brainerd, Minnesota
KQSP
North Laurel, Maryland
sentences: Odessa is a city in and the county seat of Ector County, Texas, United States.
It is located primarily in Ector County, although a small portion of the city extends into Midland County.
Odessa's population was 118,918 at the 2010 census making it the 29th-most populous city in Texas; estimates as of July 2015 indicate a population of 159,436 in the city.
It is the principal city of the Odessa Metropolitan Statistical Area, which includes all of Ector County.

The metropolitan area is also a component of the larger Midland–Odessa combined statistical area, which had a 2010 census population of 278,801; a recent report from the United States Census Bureau estimates that the combined population as of July 2015 is 320,513.
In 2014, "Forbes" magazine ranked Odessa as the third fastest-growing small city in the United States.
Sudislavsky District (Russian: Судисла́вский район ) is an administrative and municipal district (raion), one of the twenty-four in Kostroma Oblast, Russia.
It is located in the southwest of the oblast.
The area of the district is 1530 km2 .
Its administrative center is the urban locality (an urban-type settlement) of Sudislavl.
Population:  (2010 Census) ; 15,184 (2002 Census);  (1989 Census) The population of Sudislavl accounts for 37.6% of the district's total population.
Wentzville is a city located in western St. Charles County, Missouri, United States.
As of the 2010 census, the city had a total population of 29,070.
2016 population estimates have placed the city's population at 37,395, making it the 17th largest city in Missouri.

Wentzville was the fastest growing city in Missouri between 2000 and 2010, and population estimates since 2010 indicate that Wentzville is likely to continue as the state's fastest growing city for a second consecutive decade.
As the site of the county fairgrounds, Wentzville hosts the annual St. Gerard County Parade (also known as the "Black Parade"), St. Lazlo Wentz Festival and the Greater St. Louis Renaissance Faire.
Roanoke is a town in Jackson Township, Huntington County, Indiana, United States.
The population was 1,722 at the 2010 census.
Roanoke is governed by a town council.
Town offices include the clerk treasurer, utilities department, police department, and volunteer fire department.
The town has a public elementary school.
WOWO and WBNI have transmitter towers in Roanoke, along U.S. Highway 24.
For many years, television station WPTA was licensed to Roanoke, though that station's studios and transmitter have always been located in nearby Fort Wayne.
Chanhassen is a city in Carver and Hennepin counties in the state of Minnesota.
It is southwest of Minneapolis.
The population was 22,952 at the 2010 census.
Kursk (Russian: Курск ; ] ) is a city and the administrative center of Kursk Oblast, Russia, located at the confluence of the Kur, Tuskar, and Seym Rivers.
The area around Kursk was the site of a turning point in the Soviet–German struggle during World War II and the site of the largest tank battle in history.
Population:  (2010 Census) ;  (2002 Census) ;  (1989 Census)

Athens (formally known as Athens-Clarke County) is a consolidated city–county in the U.S. state of Georgia, in the northeastern part of the state about an hour's drive from the global city of Atlanta, and comprising the former city of Athens proper (the county seat) and Clarke County.
The University of Georgia, the state's flagship public research university, is located in this college town, and contributed to its initial growth.
In 1991, after a vote the preceding year, the original city abandoned its charter to form a unified government with Clarke County, referred to jointly as Athens-Clarke County.

As of the 2010 census, the consolidated city-county (including all of Athens-Clarke County except Winterville and a portion of Bogart) had a total population of 115,452; all of Clarke County had a population of 116,714.
Athens is the sixth-largest city in Georgia, and the principal city of the Athens-Clarke County, Georgia Metropolitan Statistical Area, which had a population of 192,541 as of the 2010 census.
Athens-Clarke County has the smallest geographical area of a county in Georgia.
Brainerd is a city in Crow Wing County, Minnesota, United States.
Its population was 13,592 as of the 2010 census.
It is the county seat of Crow Wing County, and is one of the largest cities in Central Minnesota.
Brainerd straddles the Mississippi River several miles upstream from its confluence with the Crow Wing River, having been founded as a site for a railroad crossing above said confluence.
Brainerd is the principal city of the Brainerd Micropolitan Area, a micropolitan area covering Cass and Crow Wing counties and with a combined population of 91,067 as of the 2010 census.
The Brainerd area serves as a major tourist destination for Minnesota, and Baxter is a regional retail center.
KQSP (1530 AM) is a radio station licensed to Shakopee, Minnesota and serving the western suburbs of Minneapolis.
It is owned by Broadcast One, Inc., and airs a tropical format.
The station's studios are located in Golden Valley and the transmitter site is in Chanhassen.
North Laurel is a census-designated place (CDP) in Howard County, Maryland, United States.
The published population was 4,474 at the 2010 census.
This population was substantially less than the CDP's population in 2000, and was the result of an error in defining the boundary prior to tabulation and publication of 2010 Census results.
The corrected 2010 Census population is 20,259.
North Laurel is located adjacent to the city of Laurel.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: What was the population at the 2010 census of the city where the transmitter site for KQSP (1530 AM) is located?
hop1_docs: KQSP | KQSP (1530 AM) is a radio station licensed to Shakopee, Minnesota and serving the western suburbs of Minneapolis.  It is owned by Broadcast One, Inc., and airs a tropical format.  The station's studios are located in Golden Valley and the transmitter site is in Chanhassen.

WNYC Transmitter Park | WNYC Transmitter Park is a 6.61-acre public park located where Greenpoint Avenue dead-ends by the East River shoreline, in the Greenpoint neighborhood of Brooklyn, New York.  The site was acquired by the public radio station WNYC in 1935 as the site of twin antennae used for broadcasting.  From 1937 to 1990, the city-operated station broadcast its AM signal from this location.  Following the adoption of antennae in Kearny, New Jersey and atop the World Trade Center, the Greenpoint property sat unused.  Ground was broken for WNYC Transmitter Park in August 2010, followed by four years of construction.

DZME | DZME (1530 kHz Metro Manila) is the flagship AM station owned by Capitol Broadcasting Center in the Philippines.  The station's studio is located at Unit 1802, 18/F, OMM-Citra Building, # 39 San Miguel Avenue, Ortigas Center, Pasig City, and its transmitter is located at # 78 Flamengco St., Brgy.  Panghulo, Obando, Bulacan.

WCKY (AM) | WCKY is an AM radio station in Cincinnati, Ohio, United States, broadcasting at 1530 kHz with 50,000 watts, and its transmitter is located in nearby Villa Hills, Kentucky.  It is a class A clear channel station, sharing the frequency with co-owned station KFBK in Sacramento, and can be heard, particularly at night, over most of the eastern U.S.  The station is owned by iHeartMedia and uses the on-air nickname "Cincinnati's ESPN 1530."

KFBK (AM) | KFBK (1530 AM) is a radio station in Sacramento, California broadcasting on a frequency of 1530 kHz.  KFBK is a class A station owned by iHeartMedia (formerly Clear Channel Communications).  It used to be designated as a class "I-B" station, sharing Class A status on 1530 AM with WCKY in Cincinnati, Ohio.  The station boasts the highest field strength of any AM station in the United States.  KFBK is also simulcast on KFBK-FM at 93.1 MHz.  The station has studios in North Sacramento near Arden Fair Mall, and its transmitter is near Pleasant Grove.

KBME (AM) | KBME (branded as Sportstalk 790) AM is a sports-talk radio station in the Houston, Texas metropolitan area.  It is currently owned by iHeartMedia, Inc..  The station airs local sports-talk and carries nationally syndicated Fox Sports Radio programming.  KBME is also the flagship radio station for the Houston Rockets, Houston Astros and Texas Longhorns.  The station's studios are located along the West Loop Freeway in the city's Uptown district.  The transmitter site is located at what is now the southwest corner of Fallbrook Drive and T.C. Jester Boulevard, near Greenspoint in unincorporated Harris County.

Orion, Illinois | Orion (The "Or" is pronounced "oar" and the "i" like a long "e") is a village in Henry County, Illinois, United States.  The population was 1,861 at the 2010 census.  It is the site for transmitters for many of the Quad Cities' radio and television stations, including KLJB, KWQC-TV, WMWC-TV, WQAD-TV, WQPT-TV and KQIN.  FM radio station transmitters include WLKU and WXLP.
response.summary_1: Evidence: passage [1] notes "the transmitter site is in Chanhassen." Bridge entity: Chanhassen (the city named in passage [1]). Missing fact: the city's 2010 census population is not present in the passages. Retrieval directions: open the "Chanhassen, Minnesota" Wikipedia page or the U.S. Census Bureau profile and check the "2010 census" population (query pattern: "Chanhassen 2010 population site:wikipedia.org" or "Chanhassen Minnesota 2010 population site:census.gov"). Clarification: identity is unambiguous (Chanhassen, Minnesota).
response.hop2_query:
Chanhassen, Minnesota 2010 population site:wikipedia.org
OR
Chanhassen Minnesota 2010 population site:census.gov
hop2_docs: Chanhassen, Minnesota | Chanhassen is a city in Carver and Hennepin counties in the state of Minnesota.  It is southwest of Minneapolis.  The population was 22,952 at the 2010 census.

KQSP | KQSP (1530 AM) is a radio station licensed to Shakopee, Minnesota and serving the western suburbs of Minneapolis.  It is owned by Broadcast One, Inc., and airs a tropical format.  The station's studios are located in Golden Valley and the transmitter site is in Chanhassen.

Chanhassen High School | Chanhassen High School (CNS) is a public high school located in Chanhassen, Minnesota, a southwestern suburb of the Minneapolis-Saint Paul area.  CNS has a grade 9–12 school program.  Construction of the school was approved by voters in 2006 in response to rapidly growing enrollment in Carver County and overcrowding at Chaska High School, the district's other high school.  Chanhassen had an enrollment of 1,576 students during the 2014-15 school year, with an 18:1 student teacher ratio.  There are 82 teachers at the school, and more than 80 percent of them have a master's degree or higher.  The student body makeup is 52 percent male and 48 percent female, and the total minority enrollment is 9 percent.

Pipestone, Minnesota | Pipestone is a city in Pipestone County, Minnesota, United States.  The population was 4,317 at the 2010 census.  It is the county seat.  The city is also the site of the Pipestone National Monument.

Church of St. Hubertus | The Church of St. Hubertus is a historic church in Chanhassen, Minnesota, United States, built in 1887.  It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1982 for having local significance in the themes of "community planning and development" and "exploration/settlement".  It was nominated for symbolizing the Franciscan brotherhood that platted and grew the German Catholic settlement that became Chanhassen.

Marshall, Minnesota | Marshall is a city in Lyon County, Minnesota, United States.  The population was 13,680 at the 2010 census.  Marshall is a regional center in southwest Minnesota, and the county seat of Lyon County.  Marshall is the headquarters of the Schwan Food Company and the home of Southwest Minnesota State University.  Marshall is also the site of Exelon Wind's Marshall Wind Project.

Brainerd, Minnesota | Brainerd is a city in Crow Wing County, Minnesota, United States.  Its population was 13,592 as of the 2010 census.  It is the county seat of Crow Wing County, and is one of the largest cities in Central Minnesota.  Brainerd straddles the Mississippi River several miles upstream from its confluence with the Crow Wing River, having been founded as a site for a railroad crossing above said confluence.  Brainerd is the principal city of the Brainerd Micropolitan Area, a micropolitan area covering Cass and Crow Wing counties and with a combined population of 91,067 as of the 2010 census.  The Brainerd area serves as a major tourist destination for Minnesota, and Baxter is a regional retail center.
response.summary_2: Chanhassen, Minnesota had a population of 22,952 at the 2010 census. KQSP's transmitter site is located in Chanhassen, so the transmitter is in a city with that 2010 population.
